CC5.2.description: Audit logging: API server not found (managed cluster)

CC6.1.description: RBAC ClusterRoleBinding inventory and cluster-admin scope
Service account token mount audit across 12 namespaces

CC6.6.description: Default-deny status: 7 of 12 namespaces have default-deny policies

CC7.1.description: Audit logging: API server not found (managed cluster)

CC7.4.description: Audit logging: API server not found (managed cluster)

A1.1.CapacitySummary: The organization maintains and monitors processing capacity through Kubernetes resource management controls that prevent resource exhaustion and ensure fair scheduling across workloads. all containers have CPU and memory limits defined.
A1.1.GovernanceDetails: Capacity management gaps identified: ResourceQuotas (namespace-level aggregate limits) and LimitRanges (default container constraints) are not fully configured across all namespaces.

A1.2.AvailabilitySummary: The organization implements environmental protections to maintain workload availability and ensure controlled degradation during maintenance and failure scenarios. critical workloads run multiple replicas.
A1.2.ResilienceDetails: Workload resilience gaps identified: PodDisruptionBudgets (controlled disruption during maintenance), liveness probes (self-healing of unhealthy containers), and readiness probes (traffic management during startup and degradation) are not fully deployed across audited workloads.
A1.2.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 5 checks passing and 2 checks failing.

CC5.1.TechControlSummary: The organization selects and develops technology control activities through Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (PSS) and admission controllers. PSS provides three graduated security profiles — Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted — that enforce container security boundaries at the namespace level. Admission controllers serve as automated policy enforcement gates, validating workload configurations against these standards before deployment and preventing non-compliant resources from being created in the cluster.

CC5.2.PolicySummary: The organization deploys control activities through Pod Security Standards, which establish the expected security posture for workloads and are enforced by admission controllers at deployment time. Audit policy configuration should be reviewed to ensure comprehensive logging of security-relevant API operations, as this provides the procedural record of control enforcement.

CC6.1.AccessControlSummary: The organization enforces logical access controls through Kubernetes Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), which provides granular permission management across all cluster resources. The RBAC configuration comprises 47 ClusterRoles and 23 ClusterRoleBindings. Access to the cluster-admin role, which grants unrestricted administrative privileges, is restricted to 2 authorized bindings: system:masters (Group) and break-glass-admin (User).
CC6.1.TokenMountSummary: Service account credential exposure is controlled across all 12 audited namespaces. Automatic mounting of service account tokens is disabled, preventing unintended credential availability to workloads that do not require Kubernetes API access.

Findings.Message: 2 ClusterRoles use wildcard resources: legacy-admin, dev-full-access

CC6.1.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 16 checks passing and 2 checks failing.

CC6.2.ProvisioningSummary: The organization manages system credential provisioning through Kubernetes service accounts and role-based access bindings. Prior to granting access, users and automated workloads are assigned dedicated service accounts with permissions scoped to their operational requirements.

CC6.3.ScopingSummary: The organization enforces role-based access and least privilege through Kubernetes RBAC, which assigns permissions based on operational roles rather than individual identity. Access permissions are scoped to minimize the impact of compromised credentials: 80% of role bindings (93 of 116) are restricted to specific namespaces rather than granted cluster-wide.
CC6.3.WildcardSummary: All ClusterRoles specify explicit resource and verb permissions. No wildcard (*) grants were identified, confirming that access is scoped to the minimum necessary for each role's function.

Findings.Message: 2 ClusterRoles use wildcard resources: legacy-admin, dev-full-access

CC6.3.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 5 checks passing and 3 checks failing.

CC6.6.CoverageSummary: The organization implements network segmentation controls through Kubernetes NetworkPolicies to restrict lateral movement between workloads and protect against threats from sources outside system boundaries. The cluster enforces 18 NetworkPolicies across 12 namespaces, providing ingress and egress traffic controls that limit network communication to explicitly authorized paths.
CC6.6.DefaultDenySummary: Default-deny policies establish a zero-trust baseline in 7 namespaces of 12, ensuring that only explicitly authorized network connections are permitted in those namespaces.
CC6.6.GapSummary: Namespaces without default-deny policies: staging, dev, monitoring, logging, and default.

Findings.Message: 3 namespaces missing NetworkPolicy: staging, dev, monitoring

CC6.6.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 8 checks passing and 4 checks failing.

CC6.7.TLSSummary: The organization restricts data transmission through encrypted channels for all Kubernetes API communications. As a managed cluster, TLS termination and certificate management for the API server are handled by the cloud provider, ensuring encrypted communication between clients and the control plane.
CC6.7.EtcdSummary: The etcd data store is managed by the cloud provider and is not directly accessible. Encryption in transit for the data store is an inherited control from the provider's infrastructure.

CC6.8.SecurityPosture: The organization implements controls to prevent the introduction of unauthorized or malicious software through Kubernetes Pod Security Standards and container security contexts. However, 1 container currently has in privileged mode, which grants full access to the host's resources and should be evaluated for necessity.
CC6.8.HardeningDetails: Additional container hardening measures include: containers run as non-root users; Linux capabilities are dropped to the minimum required set; Seccomp profiles restrict available system calls.

Findings.Message: 4 containers allow privilege escalation in namespace: production

CC6.8.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 22 checks passing and 3 checks failing.

CC7.1.AuditLoggingSummary: The organization maintains a continuous audit trail of all cluster infrastructure events to support anomaly detection and forensic analysis. As a managed Kubernetes cluster, audit logging is provided by the cloud provider's control plane, which captures API server requests, authentication events, and authorization decisions. The organization supplements provider-managed audit logging with runtime monitoring of workload configurations and security policy compliance through automated benchmark scanning.

Findings.Message: 3 namespaces missing NetworkPolicy: staging, dev, monitoring

CC7.1.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 12 checks passing and 2 checks failing.

CC7.2.MonitoringSummary: The organization monitors system components and their operation for anomalies through automated security scanning and workload health monitoring. A centralized compliance scanning system provides a consolidated view of the cluster's security posture, covering privileged container detection, network policy enforcement, workload health probes, and resource utilization. Automated scans assess 7 control points across these domains, providing ongoing visibility into configuration drift and security baseline deviations.

Findings.Message: 3 namespaces missing NetworkPolicy: staging, dev, monitoring

CC7.2.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 3 checks passing and 4 checks failing.

CC7.3.EvaluationSummary: The organization evaluates detected security events to determine whether they could result in a failure to meet its security objectives. Automated compliance scanning provides a centralized security dashboard with findings aggregated by severity, enabling the security team to prioritize investigation and response. Each finding includes the specific resource, namespace, and configuration detail that triggered the detection, supporting rapid triage and root cause analysis.

Findings.Message: 3 namespaces missing NetworkPolicy: staging, dev, monitoring

CC7.3.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 1 check passing and 2 checks failing.

CC7.4.IncidentReadiness: The organization maintains incident response readiness through continuous audit logging of all Kubernetes API operations, providing the forensic trail necessary to identify, evaluate, and respond to security incidents. Audit records capture the identity of actors, actions performed, resources affected, and timestamps, enabling the incident response team to reconstruct event sequences during investigation. Automated compliance scanning supplements the audit trail by continuously monitoring for configuration drift and security policy violations that may indicate an active incident or attempted compromise.

CC7.5.RecoverySummary: The organization implements recovery controls to restore normal operations after security incidents. Kubernetes provides declarative state management through its reconciliation loop, which continuously drives the cluster toward the desired state defined in resource specifications. This self-healing capability automatically restarts failed containers, reschedules workloads from unhealthy nodes, and restores configuration state from the cluster's persistent data store. Audit log retention policies ensure that forensic records are preserved to support post-incident analysis and process improvement.

CC8.1.ChangeControlSummary: The organization enforces change management controls through infrastructure-as-code practices and image provenance verification. However, 1 container currently has secrets exposed through environment variables. Environment variable secrets are visible in pod specifications and process listings; migrating to Kubernetes Secrets or an external secrets manager reduces this exposure.
CC8.1.SupplyChainSummary: Supply chain integrity controls are in place: container images are pinned to immutable digests, preventing unauthorized image substitution; Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) attestations provide supply chain transparency for deployed artifacts.

Findings.Message: 3 roles grant get/list/watch on secrets: dev-team, monitoring, ci-deploy
8 pods reference secrets via environment variables

CC8.1.AssessmentStatement: Assessment: PARTIAL — 4 checks passing and 2 checks failing.

CC5.1.description: Show Pod Security Standard labels per namespace
CC5.1.command: kubectl get namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, pss: (.metadata.labels | with_entries(select(.key | startswith("pod-security"))))}'

CC5.2.command: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o json | jq '.items[0].spec.containers[0].args[] | select(startswith("--audit-"))'

CC6.1.command: kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq '[.items[] | select(.roleRef.name=="cluster-admin")] | length'
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.roleRef.name=="cluster-admin") | {name: .metadata.name, subjects: .subjects}'

CC6.1.description: List service accounts with auto-mount enabled
CC6.1.command: kubectl get serviceaccounts -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.automountServiceAccountToken == true) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'

CC6.3.command: kubectl get clusterroles -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.rules[]? | (.resources[]? == "*") or (.verbs[]? == "*")) | .metadata.name'
echo "RoleBindings: $(kubectl get rolebindings -A --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l), ClusterRoleBindings: $(kubectl get clusterrolebindings --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)"

CC6.6.command: kubectl get namespaces -o json | jq -r '.items[].metadata.name' | while read ns; do count=$(kubectl get networkpolicies -n "$ns" --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l); [ "$count" -eq 0 ] && echo "$ns"; done
kubectl get networkpolicies -A --no-headers 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

kubectl get networkpolicies -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.spec.podSelector == {} and (.spec.ingress | length) == 0 and (.spec.egress | length) == 0) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'

CC6.7.command: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o json | jq '.items[0].spec.containers[0].args[] | select(startswith("--tls-"))'
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd -o json | jq '.items[0].spec.containers[0].args[] | select(startswith("--cert-") or startswith("--peer-cert-") or startswith("--trusted-ca-"))'

CC6.8.description: Show Pod Security Standard labels per namespace
CC6.8.command: kubectl get namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, pss: (.metadata.labels | with_entries(select(.key | startswith("pod-security"))))}'

CC7.1.command: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o json | jq '.items[0].spec.containers[0].args[] | select(startswith("--audit-"))'

CC7.4.command: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o json | jq '.items[0].spec.containers[0].args[] | select(startswith("--audit-"))'

CC8.1.command: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o json | jq '.items[0].spec.containers[0].args[] | select(startswith("--tls-"))'
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd -o json | jq '.items[0].spec.containers[0].args[] | select(startswith("--cert-") or startswith("--peer-cert-") or startswith("--trusted-ca-"))'

A1.2.description: Define liveness and readiness probes on all containers to enable self-healing and traffic management.
Create PodDisruptionBudgets for multi-replica workloads to ensure controlled maintenance and upgrades.

CC6.1.checkName: Minimize wildcard use in Roles and ClusterRoles
CC6.1.description: Remove wildcard (*) permissions from Roles and ClusterRoles. Specify explicit resources and verbs.
Remove wildcard permissions from all Roles and ClusterRoles.

CC6.3.checkName: Minimize wildcard use in Roles and ClusterRoles
CC6.3.description: Remove wildcard (*) permissions from Roles and ClusterRoles. Specify explicit resources and verbs.
Apply least-privilege RBAC policies. Replace broad permissions with specific resource/verb combinations.
Remove wildcard permissions from all Roles and ClusterRoles.

CC6.6.checkName: Ensure every namespace has a NetworkPolicy
CC6.6.description: Apply NetworkPolicies to restrict egress traffic from pods to only required destinations.
Restrict external network access to only required pods using egress NetworkPolicies.
Create egress NetworkPolicies per namespace to restrict outbound traffic to only required destinations.
Create LimitRanges in each namespace to set default container resource constraints.

CC6.8.description: Do not allow privileged containers. Set privileged: false in security contexts.

CC6.8.checkName: Ensure allowPrivilegeEscalation is set to false
CC6.8.description: Set allowPrivilegeEscalation: false in pod security contexts.
Prevent privilege escalation with allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.
